Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 47 actions; a strong score needs about 33 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 46 actions returned the board to a state already seen 46 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 46 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Good, the game is not over. I clicked the orange square and the white diamond moved there. Let me check the current state and continue visiting waypoints.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("=== Current waypoints with children ===")
for n in nodes:
    if n['children'] and n['pixels'] <= 25:
        if n['boundary']:
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            center = f"~({sum(rows)//len(rows)}, {sum(cols)//len(cols)})"
        else:
            center = "?"
        print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']} px={n['pixels']} {center}")

# Find white diamond
print("\n=== White diamond ===")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] <= 10:
        if n['boundary']:
            rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
            cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
            center = f"~({sum(rows)//len(rows)}, {sum(cols)//len(cols)})"
        else:
            center = "?"
        print(f"  id={n['id']} px={n['pixels']} {center}")

# Progress bar
print("\n=== Progress bar ===")
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] > 10:
        print(f"  px={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Let me follow the line. The line seems to go from the white diamond toward the gray square at ~(9, 49). Let me click that.
Actually, I'm not sure about the order. Let me just click the closest waypoint. The white diamond is at (12, 24). The distances to other waypoints:
- O ~(14, 27): sqrt((14-12)^2 + (27-24)^2) = sqrt(4+9) = sqrt(13) ≈ 3.6
- G ~(9, 49): sqrt((9-12)^2 + (49-24)^2) = sqrt(9+625) = sqrt(634) ≈ 25.2
- G ~(21, 8): sqrt((21-12)^2 + (8-24)^2) = sqrt(81+256) = sqrt(337) ≈ 18.4
- G ~(35, 45): sqrt((35-12)^2 + (45-24)^2) = sqrt(529+441) = sqrt(970) ≈ 31.1
- p ~(41, 49): sqrt((41-12)^2 + (49-24)^2) = sqrt(841+625) = sqrt(1466) ≈ 38.3
- G ~(48, 54): sqrt((48-12)^2 + (54-24)^2) = sqrt(1296+900) = sqrt(2196) ≈ 46.9
The closest is O ~(14, 27). Let me click it.
